lists.title: Jamaican Patois A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: First Patwa: the all-purpose pronouns, the three 'be's (a, de, nothing), bare verbs, progressive a, dem plurals, negation, questions, and articles.

lists.title: Jamaican Patois A2 Grammar Path
lists.description: Marking time: past did, futures wi/ago, completive don, fi possession and infinitives, se clauses, neva, and the modal set.

lists.title: Jamaican Patois B1 Grammar Path
lists.description: The West African engine room: serial verbs, predicate clefts, mek, reduplication, stacked markers (did a), and the preposition system.

lists.title: Jamaican Patois B2 Grammar Path
lists.description: Full sentences with attitude: conditionals, focus fronting, question tags, and existentials.

lists.title: Jamaican Patois C1 Grammar Path
lists.description: Culture in the grammar: the basilect–acrolect continuum and the proverb tradition.

lists.title: Jamaican Patois C2 Grammar Path
lists.description: The written and sacred registers: Iyaric (Rasta talk) and Cassidy/JLU orthography in literature.

points.title: Pronouns: mi, yu, im, wi, unu, dem
points.function: One form does it all — subject, object, and possessive
points.explanation: Patois pronouns don't change form: mi = I/me/my, yu = you/your, im = he/she/him/her (both genders!), i = it, wi = we/us/our, unu = you all (a real plural 'you' English lacks), dem = they/them/their. So: Mi si im (I saw him), Im si mi (She saw me), A fi mi buk (It's my book). Spelling note: this course uses the Cassidy/JLU phonemic spelling (mi, yu, di); the everyday English-based spellings (me, yuh, de) are accepted in answers.
points.culture_note: unu is one of Patois's West African inheritances (Igbo unu). Hearing 'unu' vs 'yu' instantly tells a listener whether one person or several are meant.

drills.translation: I saw her at the market.

points.title: The equative copula a: Mi a di tiicha
points.function: Say who or what someone IS
points.explanation: To link two nouns ('X is Y'), Patois uses a: Mi a di tiicha (I am the teacher), Im a dakta (she's a doctor), Dat a fi mi kyaar (that's my car). This little a is one of the busiest words in the language — it's also the progressive marker (next lessons) and the preposition 'to/at'. Context always tells you which one you're hearing.

points.title: The locative copula de: Im de a yaad
points.function: Say WHERE someone or something is
points.explanation: For location, Patois switches from a to de: Im de a yaad (he's at home), Di buk de pan di tiebl (the book is on the table), Mi de ya (I'm here — also the classic reply to 'wa gwaan'). Equation takes a, location takes de — mixing them (Mi a ya) is the classic beginner slip. De also means 'there': di buk de-so (the book is right there).

drills.translation: The food is on the table.

points.title: Zero copula with adjectives: Di uman nais
points.function: With adjectives, there is no 'is' at all
points.explanation: Before an adjective, Patois uses NO copula: Di uman nais (the woman is nice), Mi taiyad (I'm tired), Di mango dem swiit (the mangoes are sweet). Adding one (Di uman a nais) is wrong — a links nouns only. This is because Patois adjectives behave like verbs: they can take tense markers directly (Mi did taiyad — I was tired).

points.title: Bare verbs — no endings, ever
points.function: One verb form covers I/you/he and present/past
points.explanation: Patois verbs never conjugate: mi ron, yu ron, im ron, wi ron. No -s, no -ed, no -ing on the verb itself. Tense and aspect come from little markers BEFORE the verb (a, did, don, wi — each gets its own lesson) or just from context: Mi si im yeside (I saw him yesterday — si stays bare, yeside does the work). Unlearning English endings is half the battle: im ronS and im ronED are both wrong.

drills.hint: see — the adverb carries the past

drills.sentence: Di pikni dem {{answer}} rais an piiz.
drills.translation: The children eat rice and peas.

drills.sentence: Mi granny {{answer}} a maakit evri satdi.
drills.translation: My grandmother goes to the market every Saturday.

points.title: Progressive a: Mi a ron
points.function: Say what is happening right now
points.explanation: a before a verb = English '-ing': Mi a ron (I am running), Im a nyam (she's eating), Wa yu a du? (what are you doing?). No form of 'be' appears — the a does everything. Note the two a's stacking: Mi a go a toun (I'm going to town) — the first marks the -ing, the second means 'to'. The greeting Wa gwaan? is literally 'what's going on?' with gwaan absorbing its a.

drills.translation: She is talking with her mother.

points.title: Plural with dem: di pikni dem
points.function: Make plurals without -s
points.explanation: Patois marks plural by putting dem AFTER the noun (with di): di pikni dem (the children), di mango dem (the mangoes), di tiicha dem (the teachers). No -s on the noun. With numbers or 'plenty' words the plural is already clear, so dem is dropped: faiv mango, nof pikni. Dem is also 'they/them', so di man dem = 'the men' but dem man de = 'those men'.

references.title: Cassidy & Le Page, Dictionary of Jamaican English

drills.hint: the plural marker, after the noun

drills.sentence: Mi si di uman {{answer}} a chorch.
drills.translation: I saw the women at church.

points.title: Negation: no and duon
points.function: Say what isn't and what doesn't happen
points.explanation: The negator is no before the verb or marker: Mi no nuo (I don't know), Im no de ya (he isn't here), Mi naa go (I'm not going — no + a fuse into naa). Duon (don't) appears in commands: Duon du dat! (don't do that!). Kyaan is 'can't'. Double negatives are grammatical and emphatic, not wrong: Nobadi no si notn (nobody saw anything).

drills.translation: I'm not going to the party.

points.title: Questions: intonation, we, uu, wa
points.function: Ask without flipping any words
points.explanation: Yes/no questions keep statement order and just rise in pitch: Yu a kom? (are you coming?). No do-support, no inversion. Question words: wa (what), we (where — also 'which/that' as a relative), uu (who), wen (when), ou (how), umoch (how much/many), wa mek (why — literally 'what makes'). They sit at the front: We yu a go? (where are you going?), Wa mek yu bex? (why are you angry?).

drills.translation: How much does the mango cost?

points.title: Articles: di, wan, and none
points.function: The, a, and when to use nothing
points.explanation: di = 'the' (di buk, di uman). wan = 'a/an', literally 'one', used when it matters that there's one: Mi waan wan mango (I want a mango). But where English needs 'a', Patois often uses NO article at all: Im a tiicha (she's a teacher — no wan), Mi a nyam mango (I'm eating mango). General statements drop di too: Mango swiit (mangoes are sweet).

drills.translation: Give me a mango, will you?

drills.sentence: Pas mi {{answer}} buk pan di tiebl.
drills.translation: Pass me the book on the table.
I want a glass of water.

points.title: Past with did: Mi did si im
points.function: Mark the past when context isn't enough
points.explanation: When the time isn't obvious, did before the verb marks past: Mi did si im (I saw him), Im did sik (she was sick — works on adjectives too!). Deep (basilectal) Patois uses en or ben the same way. But remember: with a time word, the bare verb is preferred — Mi nyam aredi (I ate already), not 'Mi did nyam aredi' unless you're stressing it. did + a = did a (was …-ing): Im did a ron (he was running).

drills.sentence: Wi {{answer}} liv a toun wan taim.
drills.translation: We used to live in town once.

drills.sentence: Yu {{answer}} de de wen it hapn?
drills.translation: Were you there when it happened?

drills.sentence: Mi {{answer}} waan kom bot mi kudn.
drills.translation: I wanted to come but I couldn't.

points.title: Future: wi and a go: Mi ago kom
points.function: Say what will and is going to happen
points.explanation: Two futures: wi = plain 'will' (Mi wi kom — I will come), and a go / ago = 'going to' for plans and predictions (Mi ago kom — I'm going to come; It ago rien — it's going to rain). Negatives: naa go (not going to), wian? no — 'won't' is usually naa or no wi. gwain is a common variant of ago (Mi gwain tel im).

drills.translation: They are going to come to the party.
Are you going to see him tomorrow?
He is not going to like this.

points.title: Completive don: Mi don nyam
points.function: Say something is finished — already done
points.explanation: don marks completion: Mi don nyam (I've already eaten / I'm done eating), Di rien don faal (the rain has stopped), Im don gaan (he's already gone). It can front OR follow the verb phrase: Mi nyam don means 'I finished eating'. With did: Mi did don nyam wen im riich (I had already eaten when he arrived) — a pluperfect from two little words.

drills.sentence: Mi did {{answer}} kuk wen dem riich.
drills.translation: I had already cooked when they arrived.

points.title: Possession with fi: a fi mi buk
points.function: Say whose it is
points.explanation: Ownership uses fi: fi mi = mine/my, fi yu = yours, fi im = his/hers, fi dem = theirs. A fi mi buk dat (that's MY book), Di kyaar a fi im (the car is his). For noun owners, simple juxtaposition works: Jan buk (John's book), mi mada yaad (my mother's house) — no 's, no 'of'. Asking: A fi uu dis? (whose is this?).

references.title: Cassidy & Le Page, Dictionary of Jamaican English

drills.translation: My mother's house is in the country.

points.title: Se after talk and know: Im tel mi se…
points.function: Report speech and thoughts
points.explanation: After verbs of saying, knowing, thinking, and hearing, se introduces the clause — like English 'that': Im tel mi se yu gaan (he told me that you left), Mi nuo se yu rait (I know you're right), Mi ier se di shap lack (I hear the shop is closed). It comes from the verb 'say' but works as pure grammar here — even Mi se se… ('I said that…') is possible in rapid speech.

drills.sentence: Im tel mi {{answer}} yu naa kom.
drills.translation: He told me that you're not coming.

drills.sentence: Mi nuo {{answer}} yu kyan du it.
drills.translation: I know that you can do it.

drills.sentence: Mi ier {{answer}} di maakit lack tide.
drills.translation: I hear that the market is closed today.
I believe that it's going to rain.

drills.sentence: Di tiicha se {{answer}} di tes aad.
drills.translation: The teacher said that the test is hard.

points.title: Fi + verb: Mi waan fi go
points.function: To-infinitives and what you're supposed to do
points.explanation: fi before a verb is 'to': Mi waan fi go (I want to go), Im laan fi swim (she learned to swim), Notn no de fi nyam (there's nothing to eat). It also carries obligation — 'supposed to': Yu fi lisn to yu mada (you should/are supposed to listen to your mother), Im no fi de ya (he shouldn't be here). In fast speech fi often drops after waan: Mi waan go.

drills.sentence: Notn no de {{answer}} du ya so.

points.title: Neva: past negation
points.function: Say what didn't happen
points.explanation: For a past event that didn't happen, Patois prefers neva: Mi neva si im (I didn't see him — NOT 'never in my life', just plain didn't). Mi neva du it! (I didn't do it!). English speakers hear 'never' and over-read it; in Patois it's the ordinary past negative. For 'never ever', add emphasis: Mi neva-eva du dat. no + did also works: Mi no did nuo (I didn't know).

drills.translation: He didn't come to the meeting.

drills.sentence: Wi {{answer}} nuo se yu de ya.
drills.translation: We didn't know you were here.
I didn't mean to upset you.

points.title: Modals: kyan, hafi, mos, shuda
points.function: Can, must, have to, should
points.explanation: kyan = can, kyaan = can't (the vowel length matters!): Mi kyan swim / Mi kyaan swim. hafi = have to: Mi hafi go nou (I have to go). mos = must: Yu mos taste dis (you must taste this). shuda = should, kuda = could, wuda = would — these stack with did for past: Mi shuda did lisn (I should have listened). mosi = 'must be/probably': Im mosi taiyad (he must be tired).

drills.translation: I have to go to work now.
We have to finish this today.
They should listen to the elders.

points.title: Serial verbs: ron go bring it kom
points.function: Chain verbs the West African way
points.explanation: Patois strings verbs together with no 'and' or 'to': Ron go kaal im (run and call him), Tek it kom ya (bring it here — 'take it come here'), Kya di buk gaan (carry the book away). Direction verbs go and kom act like 'thither' and 'hither' at the end of the chain. This serial-verb pattern is a direct West African inheritance and one of the deepest structural differences from English.
points.culture_note: Serial verbs appear across the Atlantic creoles and their West African substrates (Akan, Ewe, Igbo) — the same 'take X come' pattern exists in Twi.

drills.sentence: Im kya di fuud {{answer}} a yaad.
drills.translation: He carried the food off home.
Run and buy bread for me.

drills.sentence: Sen di pikni {{answer}} kaal di dakta.
drills.translation: Send the child to go call the doctor.

points.title: Predicate cleft: A tiif im tiif it
points.function: Emphasize the action by doubling it
points.explanation: To stress a verb, Patois fronts a copy of it with a: A tiif im tiif it (he STOLE it — stealing is what he did), A ron mi did a ron (I was RUNNING), A waak wi waak kom (we WALKED here). The pattern is a + verb-copy + subject + verb. It answers doubt: someone questions how, and the cleft insists. Adjectives cleft too: A sik im sik (he's really SICK).

drills.translation: He STOLE the money (stealing is what he did).

drills.sentence: A {{answer}} wi waak kom a toun.

drills.translation: He is really SICK, no joke.

drills.sentence: A {{answer}} dem a ron, no waak.
A {{answer}} mi nyam di huol pat.
drills.translation: I ATE the whole pot (eating is what I did).

points.title: Mek: let, make, and let's
points.function: Suggest, permit, and cause with one verb
points.explanation: mek covers make, let, and let's: Mek wi go (let's go), Mek mi si (let me see), Yu a mek mi liet (you're making me late), Mek im gwaan (let him carry on). Wa mek…? (why — 'what makes'). In causatives the pattern is mek + pronoun + verb, with everything bare as usual. Duon mek dem fuul yu (don't let them fool you).

drills.sentence: Yu a {{answer}} mi liet fi wok.
drills.translation: You're making me late for work.

points.title: Reduplication: likl-likl, was-was
points.function: Double a word to change its meaning
points.explanation: Doubling is productive grammar. With adjectives it intensifies or attenuates: gud-gud (very good), likl-likl (bit by bit), swiit-swiit (really sweet). With verbs it means repeated/ongoing action: taak-taak (talk on and on), luk-luk (keep looking around). Some doubled forms are their own words: was-was (wasps' nest area), fenke-fenke (feeble), soso (only/plain — soso rais, plain rice).

references.title: Cassidy & Le Page, Dictionary of Jamaican English

drills.translation: Eat it little by little, don't rush.

drills.sentence: Im de de a {{answer}}-taak all die.
drills.translation: He's there talking on and on all day.

drills.sentence: Wa mek yu a {{answer}}-luk pan mi so?
drills.translation: Why do you keep looking at me like that?

drills.sentence: Mi onggl av {{answer}} rais fi nyam.
drills.translation: I only have plain rice to eat.

points.title: Continuous past: Im did a ron
points.function: Was doing — stack the markers
points.explanation: The preverbal markers combine in fixed order: TENSE before ASPECT. did + a = was …-ing: Im did a ron (he was running), Wi did a nyam wen yu kaal (we were eating when you called). With the future: wi + a? No — future continuous is ago + verb or wi de + verb regionally. The full stack: did + don + a is rare but grammatical (did don a nyam — had already been eating). Learn did-a as one unit and the system opens up.

drills.sentence: Im did {{answer}} slip wen mi riich.
drills.translation: He was sleeping when I arrived.

drills.sentence: Wi {{answer}} a kuk wen di lait gaan.
drills.translation: We were cooking when the power went.

drills.sentence: Dem did {{answer}} plie futbaal huol die.
drills.translation: They were playing football all day.

drills.sentence: Mi {{answer}} a taak to im wen yu kaal.
drills.translation: I was talking to him when you called.

drills.sentence: Di rien did {{answer}} faal aal nait.
drills.translation: The rain was falling all night.

points.title: Prepositions: ina, pan, outa
points.function: In, on, and out with Patois's own flavour
points.explanation: ina = in/inside/into: ina di yaad (in the yard), ina di maanin (in the morning). pan = on/upon: pan di tiebl (on the table), also 'at' someone: no luk pan mi (don't look at me). outa = out of: outa di ous (out of the house), also 'from': im kom outa Kingston. a alone covers 'to/at' for places: go a skuul, de a chorch. Time phrases lean on ina and pan where English switches: pan Sondi (on Sunday).

drills.sentence: Di pikni dem de {{answer}} di yaad.
drills.translation: The children are in the yard.
Put the book on the table.
We go to church on Sunday.
Don't look at me like that.

drills.sentence: Im waak {{answer}} di ous vex vex.
drills.translation: He walked out of the house very angry.

points.title: Conditionals: ef … wuda
points.function: Ifs, woulds, and would-haves
points.explanation: Real condition: ef + plain clause: Ef it rien, wi stie a yaad (if it rains, we stay home). Unreal: ef + did …, wuda + verb: Ef mi did nuo, mi wuda kom (if I had known, I would have come). wuda did also appears for the 'would have': Mi wuda did tel yu. The tense logic rides entirely on did and wuda — no subjunctive forms, no 'were'.

drills.translation: If it rains, we're not going.

drills.sentence: Ef mi did nuo, mi {{answer}} kom.
drills.translation: If I had known, I would have come.

drills.sentence: Ef yu {{answer}} lisn, yu wudn ina problem.
drills.translation: If you had listened, you wouldn't be in trouble.
drills.hint: the past marker in the if-clause

drills.sentence: Mi {{answer}} bai it ef mi av moni.
drills.translation: I would buy it if I had money.

drills.sentence: Ef im {{answer}} ya, im wuda laaf.
drills.translation: If he had been here, he would have laughed.

drills.sentence: Wa yu {{answer}} du ef yu win di moni?
drills.translation: What would you do if you won the money?

points.title: Focus fronting: A we yu a go?
points.function: Point the spotlight with a
points.explanation: The same a that clefts verbs fronts ANY focused element: A we yu a go? (where exactly are you going?), A yu mi a taak to (it's YOU I'm talking to), A tide di maakit kip (it's today the market is held), A fi mi it a fi mi! (it's MINE!). The pattern is a + focused-thing + rest of sentence, and it's everywhere in real speech — questions, accusations, corrections.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} yu mi a taak to, no im.
drills.translation: It's YOU I'm talking to, not him.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} we yu did de huol die?
drills.translation: Where exactly were you all day?

drills.sentence: {{answer}} mi kuk di dina, no yu.
drills.translation: It was ME who cooked the dinner, not you.
It's today the bus comes early.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} dat mi did a tel yu!
drills.translation: THAT's what I was telling you!

points.title: Question tags: no chuu? iihn? no?
points.function: Pull the listener in
points.explanation: Patois seasons statements with agreement-seeking tags: no chuu? (isn't it true?), iihn? (right?/eh?), no? and nuh at the end soften or invite: Yu a kom, no? (you're coming, right?), Di fuud gud, iihn? Sentence-final man softens or emphasizes among peers (Chill, man). Sentence-final yaa/ya adds warmth to advice: Tek kier a yuself, yaa. These particles carry the social temperature of the sentence.

drills.translation: The mangoes are sweet, aren't they?
Take care of yourself, you hear?
We'll see you tomorrow, won't we?

points.title: Existentials and weather: it av, wan … de
points.function: There is, there are, and what the sky is doing
points.explanation: 'There is/are' has several forms: it av (it have): It av wan shap dong di ruod (there's a shop down the road); de + subject: Nof mango de pan di tri (there are lots of mangoes on the tree); or bare de-so constructions. Weather runs on verbs without 'it': Rien a faal (it's raining — 'rain is falling'), Son at (the sun is hot). Negative existential: notn no de (there's nothing).

drills.sentence: It {{answer}} wan gud shap a toun.
drills.translation: There's a good shop in town.
There are lots of people at the party.

drills.sentence: Notn no {{answer}} fi nyam ina di ous.
drills.translation: There's nothing to eat in the house.

drills.sentence: It av {{answer}} riva baka di bush.
drills.translation: There's a river behind the bush.
The sun is very hot today.

points.title: The continuum: Patwa to Jamaican English
points.function: Slide the register like a local
points.explanation: Jamaican speech lives on a continuum from deep basilect (Im en a nyam) through mesolect (Im did a eat) to Jamaican Standard English (He was eating). Speakers shift by situation: yard and market talk sits deep, radio interviews sit high, and most daily speech floats between. The skill is HEARING where an utterance sits and matching it: answering deep Patwa in Standard English reads as distant; going deep in a job interview misfires. Watch the tells: en vs did vs was; nyam vs eat; unu vs you all; th-sounds appearing as the register rises (ting → thing).
points.culture_note: The continuum is why 'is Patois broken English?' misses the point — it's a system with its own grammar that speakers navigate PLUS English, a bilingual skill, not a failed monolingual one.

drills.sentence: Deep Patwa fi 'he was eating': Im en a {{answer}}.
drills.translation: Deep Patois for 'he was eating': Im en a nyam.

drills.sentence: Mid-register: Im did a {{answer}} di rais.
drills.translation: Mid-register: he was eating the rice.

drills.sentence: Deep past marker: Mi {{answer}} si im.
drills.translation: Deep-register: I saw him. (en = basilect did)

drills.hint: th returns as you go up

points.title: Proverbs: Wanti wanti kyaan getti
points.function: Carry an argument in one inherited sentence
points.explanation: Jamaican proverbs compress the culture. Wanti wanti kyaan getti, getti getti no wanti (those who want can't get; those who get don't want). Wan wan koko ful baskit (one by one fills the basket — patience). Chikin merry, hawk de near (rejoicing invites danger). Duppy know who fi frighten (bullies pick their targets). Yu shake man han, yu no shake im haat (a handshake isn't trust). Proverbs deploy mid-argument as complete reasoning — learn them whole, with their rhythm.

references.title: Cassidy & Le Page, Dictionary of Jamaican English

drills.translation: One by one (cocoyam by cocoyam) fills the basket.
drills.hint: the root vegetable in the patience proverb

drills.translation: When the chicken is merry, the hawk is near.
The ghost knows whom to frighten.

drills.sentence: Yu shiek man an, yu no shiek im {{answer}}.
drills.translation: You shake a man's hand, not his heart.
Every little bit makes a lot. (every mickle makes a muckle)

points.title: Iyaric: I-an-I and Rasta wordcraft
points.function: Understand Rastafari's deliberate remaking of the language
points.explanation: Rastafari reshaped Jamaican speech on principle. I-an-I replaces mi/wi — the self and Jah are one, so the objectified 'me' is rejected. 'Downpressor' replaces 'oppressor' (why would pressing DOWN be 'op'?); 'overstand' replaces 'understand'; 'livication' replaces 'dedication' (no 'dead' in a living act). I- prefixing sacralizes: Ital (pure, natural food), Irie (blessed harmony), Iditation (meditation). This is conscious philosophy of language — sound-symbolism as theology — and it flows through reggae into global speech.
points.culture_note: Treat Iyaric with respect: it's religious register, not slang. Using irie or ital casually is fine (they've entered general Patois); imitating I-an-I speech as an outsider can read as mockery.

references.title: Cassidy & Le Page, Dictionary of Jamaican English

drills.sentence: Rasta seh: {{answer}} a go a di hills.
drills.translation: Rasta says: I (we) am going to the hills.
Pure natural food is ital food.
Everything is irie — blessed and in harmony.

drills.sentence: Wi a huol wan {{answer}} pan di riddim.
drills.translation: We're holding a meditation on the riddim.

points.title: Written Patwa: Cassidy spelling and dialect literature
points.function: Read Miss Lou, dub poetry, and the JLU Bible
points.explanation: Written Patois has two live conventions. The Cassidy/JLU system (used in this course) is phonemic: one sound, one spelling — di, likl, kyaan, uman. Ad-hoc English-based spelling is what most people actually type: de/di, likkle, cyaan, ooman. Louise Bennett-Coverley ('Miss Lou') legitimized written Patois with English-based spellings ('Colonization in Reverse'); dub poets (Linton Kwesi Johnson, Mutabaruka) push phonemic choices for political point; Di Jamiekan Nyuu Testiment (2012) is full Cassidy-JLU. Reading fluently means holding both systems — and noticing what each choice signals.

references.title: Cassidy & Le Page, Dictionary of Jamaican English

drills.sentence: Di Jamiekan Nyuu {{answer}} yuuz Cassidy-JLU spelling.
drills.translation: The Jamaican New Testament uses Cassidy-JLU spelling.

drills.sentence: Miss Lou riyal niem a Louise {{answer}}.
drills.translation: Miss Lou's real name is Louise Bennett.
